title: Grilled cheese sandwich
subtitle: Cheddar melted between buttered bread, pressed golden on the hob
excerpt: The American diner staple: two slices of white bread, buttered on the outside, filled with cheddar, and cooked in a pan over medium heat until the bread is deeply golden and the cheese runs. The whole thing takes seven minutes and is better than it has any right to be.
sourceNotes: The grilled cheese sandwich entered American diner culture in the 1920s and 1930s, as processed American cheese became widely available and affordable. Cheese-and-bread toast preparations appear in American domestic cookery from the late nineteenth century, documented in Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544). The open-faced cheese toast became the closed sandwich by the 1940s, widely served in school cafeterias and diners.

content.text: A grilled cheese sandwich is one of the few dishes where technique matters more than ingredients. The bread must be buttered on the outside (not the inside) to brown properly. The heat must be medium, not high: high heat chars the surface before the cheese melts. The cheese must be in direct contact with the bread on both sides so it melts evenly rather than pooling in the centre. These three things done correctly produce a sandwich that is crackled and golden outside and molten inside.

Cheddar is the standard choice. American diner-style processed cheese (the plastic-wrapped singles) melts more evenly and produces the cleaner, stretchier pull. Aged cheddar has more flavour but tends to separate slightly at high heat. Use what you have, or blend the two. The bread should be white sandwich bread: thick-cut farmhouse loaf or standard sliced, but not sourdough or rye, which have too much texture to butter and press flat.

items.name: Cheddar
items.prepNote: grated or thinly sliced; American cheese singles or mature cheddar

content.text: Butter the bread and fill with cheese

Spread the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter evenly over one side of each bread slice, covering the surface right to the edges, any unbuttered spot will not brown. Lay two slices butter-side down on a chopping board. Arrange the {{cheddar}} of cheddar evenly over both, making sure the cheese extends to the edges of the bread. Place the remaining slices on top, butter-side up.

Set the frying pan over medium heat. When the pan is warm (not smoking), place the sandwiches in. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes without touching until the underside is deep golden and the cheese at the edges has started to soften. Flip carefully with a fish slice. Cook for a further 2 to 3 minutes until the second side matches. The cheese should be fully melted through. Rest for 30 seconds before cutting.

items.fix: Use medium heat only; cover the pan for 30 seconds after flipping to trap heat and melt the cheese faster without further browning the bread
items.symptom: Bread is burnt but cheese is not melted

items.fix: The pan should be warm before the sandwich goes in; ensure the butter covers the full surface of the bread, unbuttered areas do not brown
items.cause: Heat too low, or butter not spread to the edges

items.fix: Keep the cheese just within the border of the bread; a little overflow is expected but excess cheese scorches in the pan
items.cause: Too much cheese, or cheese extends too far beyond the bread

items.fix: Use a blend of aged and processed cheese; do not cook past the point where the cheese is fully melted at the edges
items.cause: Overcooked or aged cheese with high fat content that separates under sustained heat
items.symptom: Sandwich is dry and the cheese has separated

content.text: The grilled cheese sandwich became an American staple as processed cheese became affordable and ubiquitous in the 1920s and 1930s. By the 1940s it was a school-cafeteria standard, served with a bowl of tinned tomato soup (a pairing that still has the grip of a rule rather than a suggestion). The dish requires nothing (no special equipment, no unusual ingredients) and is demonstrably greater than the sum of its parts. It is the dish that American cooks make when they want something reliable and good, at any age, at any time of day.
